nodes.name: Kutsa, Puru, Śatadyumna, Tapasvī, Satyavāk, Kavi, Agniṣṭut, Atirātra, Sudymna, Abhimanyu
Aṅga, Sumanas, Svāti, Kratu, Aṅgiras, Maya
Svayambhuva Manu's sons (names: Agniidhra, Agnibahu, Medhya, Medhatithi, Vasu, Jyotishman, Dyutiman, Havya, Savala, Putrasamjnaka)
Svayambhuva Manu's seven sages (names: Marici, Atri, Angiras, Pulaha, Kratu, Pulastya, Vasistha)

edges.dst_name: Kutsa, Puru, Śatadyumna, Tapasvī, Satyavāk, Kavi, Agniṣṭut, Atirātra, Sudymna, Abhimanyu
Kutsa, Puru, Śatadyumna, Tapasvī, Satyavāk, Kavi, Agniṣṭut, Atirātra, Sudymna, Abhimanyu

edges.rel: will be born as son of

beats.title: The Setting of the Naimiṣāraṇya Sacrifice
beats.summary: The chapter describes the setting of the Naimiṣāraṇya forest, where sages perform a twelve-year sacrifice. The sage Sūta Lomaharṣaṇa arrives and is honored. The sages ask him to narrate the creation and dissolution of the universe, praising Viṣṇu as the supreme cause.

beats.title: Cosmic Creation from the Golden Egg
beats.summary: The chapter describes the creation of the universe by the self-born Brahma, who first created waters, then the golden egg from which Brahma himself was born. He split the egg into heaven and earth, created the mind-born sages (Marici, Atri, Angiras, Pulastya, Pulaha, Kratu, Vasistha), Rudra, and Sanatkumara. It also explains the etymology of Narayana and the origin of the Vedas.

beats.title: The Creation and Genealogy of Manu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the creation of beings by Prajapati (Brahma) and the lineage of Manu, including the birth of Dhruva and his descendants. It details the division of Prajapati's body into male and female, the creation of Virat and Viraja, and the genealogy from Svayambhuva Manu through Dhruva to the Cakshusha Manu.

beats.title: The Lineage of Prithu and the Pracetasas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the lineage of Prithu Vainya, the first king anointed with the Rajasuya sacrifice, and the subsequent generations including the Pracetasas. It narrates how Prithu milked the earth for the benefit of all beings, and how the Pracetasas, after long penance under the ocean, destroyed the overgrown trees with wind and fire, until Soma intervened and offered them a maiden (the daughter of the trees) as a wife.

beats.title: The Origin and Creation of Daksha
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana narrates the origin of Daksha Prajapati, his birth from Brahma's thumb, his marriage to Asikni, and his creation of various beings. It also explains how Daksha became a Prachetasa (son of the Pracetas) and the husband of his own granddaughter, Māriṣā, through a complex lineage involving Soma and the Pracetas. The chapter emphasizes the cyclical nature of creation and the importance of tapas (austerity).

beats.title: The Destruction of Daksha's Sons and the Marriage of His Daughters
beats.summary: Narada, the divine sage, recounts how he previously caused the destruction of Daksha's sons (Haryashvas and Shabalashvas) by advising them to explore the earth's limits before procreating. Later, Daksha, fearing Narada's influence, gives his daughter to Brahma, from whom Narada is reborn. Daksha then creates sixty daughters, who are given as wives to various beings including Kashyapa, Dharma, Soma, and others, leading to the birth of various deities.

beats.title: Genealogy of the Vasus, Rudras, and Wives of Kashyapa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the progeny of the Vasus, the birth of the Rudras, and the wives of Kashyapa, listing various deities and their offspring. It also mentions the Tuṣita gods and their decision to enter Aditi in the Vaivasvata Manvantara.

beats.title: Genealogy of the Adityas, Daityas, and Danavas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the lineage of the Adityas, the sons of Diti and Danu, and the descendants of Hiranyakashipu and Hiranyaksha, including the Daityas and Danavas. It recounts the birth of the twelve Adityas from Aditi and Kashyapa, the progeny of Soma's wives, and the genealogies of the demon clans, culminating in the list of Danava chiefs led by Vipracitti.

beats.title: Genealogies of Danavas, Daityas, and Nagas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana (part 9) enumerates the lineages of Danavas, Daityas, Nagas, and other beings, detailing their origins from Kashyapa and various mothers. It lists prominent figures such as Vipracitti, Svarbhanu, Puloma, and their descendants, including the Nivatakavacas, and describes the progeny of Tamra, Vinata, Surasa, and Kadru, culminating in the major Nagas.

beats.title: The Birth of the Maruts and the Glory of Hari
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the lineage of beings born from Kashyapa and his wives, focusing on the creation of various species and the story of Diti's pregnancy. Diti, seeking a son to kill Indra, receives a boon from Kashyapa but breaks her vow of purity, allowing Indra to enter her womb and cut the fetus into forty-nine pieces, which become the Maruts. The chapter concludes with praise of Hari (Vishnu) and the coronation of Prithu.

beats.title: The Coronation of Prithu and the Appointment of Cosmic Rulers
beats.summary: This chapter describes the coronation of King Prithu by Brahma and the subsequent appointment of various deities and beings as rulers over different domains. It also recounts the transition from the Chakshusha Manvantara to the Vaivasvata Manvantara, and sets the stage for the detailed narration of Prithu's birth and the milking of the earth.

beats.title: The Story of King Vena and the Birth of Pṛthu
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the story of King Vena, a wicked ruler who defied dharma and the sages. The sages, led by Atri, killed him by churning his thigh, producing a dark dwarf (Niṣāda) and then churned his right arm to produce King Pṛthu, a righteous ruler.

beats.title: The Birth and Reign of King Prithu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana narrates the birth of King Prithu from the churning of King Vena's right hand, his coronation, the origin of the Suta and Magadha bards, and Prithu's pursuit of the earth in the form of a cow to provide sustenance for his subjects.

beats.title: Prithu Milks the Earth
beats.summary: King Vainya (Prithu) confronts the Earth goddess, who initially refuses to yield her resources. After a debate on dharma, she agrees to become his daughter and allows him to milk her using Manu as the calf, establishing agriculture and settlement.

beats.title: The Cosmic Milking of the Earth by Prithu
beats.summary: This chapter describes the cosmic milking of the Earth by various beings, led by King Prithu Vainya, who milked the Earth to provide sustenance. It lists the different groups (deities, sages, ancestors, etc.) and their respective milkers, calves, vessels, and milk products, symbolizing the diverse yields of the Earth. The chapter concludes by praising Prithu as the ideal king and source of prosperity.

beats.title: Chapter 0 (part 16)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the enumeration of the fourteen Manvantaras (ages of Manu), starting with Svayambhuva Manu and listing subsequent Manus, their sons, the seven sages, and the deities for each period. It also recounts the lineage of Prithu and the importance of honoring him.

beats.title: Enumeration of Manvantaras
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the Manvantaras (ages of Manu), listing the seven sages, deities, and sons for each Manu, including the current Vaivasvata Manvantara and the future Savarni Manvantara. It emphasizes the cyclical nature of time and the role of sages in maintaining dharma.

beats.title: Future Manvantaras and the Origin of the Sun
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the future Manvantaras (ages of Manu), including the seven future Manus: Sāvarṇi, Raucya, Bhautya, and others. It also narrates the origin of Vivasvān (the Sun) from Kaśyapa and Aditi, his marriage to Saṃjñā, and the birth of Mārtaṇḍa (another name for the Sun). The chapter emphasizes the cyclical nature of creation and dissolution, and the ultimate refuge in Hari Nārāyaṇa.

beats.title: The Story of Saṃjñā and Vivasvat
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana narrates the story of Vivasvat (the Sun god) and his wife Saṃjñā. Unable to bear the intense brilliance of her husband, Saṃjñā creates a shadow self (Chāyā) to take her place and goes to her father's house, eventually turning into a mare. Vivasvat, unaware, fathers children with Chāyā, including Manu Sāvarṇi and Śanaiścara (Saturn). Saṃjñā's partiality towards her own son causes conflict, leading Yama to kick her and receive a curse that his legs will fall off. Vivasvat intervenes, but the curse is mitigated rather than nullified.

beats.title: The Story of Vivasvan and Samjna: Birth of Yama, Yamuna, Ashvins, and Others
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana narrates the story of Vivasvan (the sun god) and his wife Samjna. Samjna, unable to bear the intense brilliance of her husband, creates a shadow wife (Chhaya) and goes to the forest to perform penance in the form of a mare. Vivasvan, after discovering the truth, seeks help from Tvashtr, who reduces his excessive tejas (brilliance) by shaving it off. From the shaved-off tejas, Vishnu's discus is created. Vivasvan then finds Samjna in her mare form, and through a union, the Ashvini twins are born. The chapter also mentions the birth of Yama, Yami (Yamuna), Manu Savarni, and Shanaishcara (Saturn), and their respective roles.

beats.title: The Story of Ila and Sudyumna
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana narrates the story of Ila, who was born as a daughter to Manu Vaivasvata from a sacrifice intended for sons. Ila, being born from the essence of Mitra and Varuna, chooses to go to them rather than follow Manu. Mitra and Varuna bless her, promising she will be known as a maiden and later become a son named Sudyumna. On her way back, she is approached by Budha (son of Soma) and bears him a son, Pururavas. After that, she becomes Sudyumna. Sudyumna has three sons: Utkala, Gaya, and Vinatashva. The chapter also describes the division of the earth among Manu's sons, with Ikshvaku receiving the central region, and Sudyumna's kingdom being given to Pururavas.

beats.title: Lineages of Kings and the Tale of Kakudmin
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana traces the lineages of various kings and sages, including the descendants of Manu, Ikshvaku, and others. It describes the story of Kakudmin (Raivata) who goes to Brahmaloka and returns to find many yugas have passed, then marries his daughter Revati to Baladeva. It also mentions the dispersal of the Sharyata clan due to rakshasas and the transformation of some kshatriyas into other varnas.

beats.title: The Slaying of Dhundhu by Kuvalashva
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana narrates the lineage of the Ikshvaku dynasty, focusing on King Kuvalashva who is tasked by the sage Uttanka to kill the demon Dhundhu. The story details the genealogy from Ikshvaku to Kuvalashva, including the origin of the name 'Shashada' and the founding of Shravasti. Uttanka explains that Dhundhu, a powerful demon, disrupts his penance and that Kuvalashva is destined to slay him, as blessed by Vishnu.

beats.title: The Slaying of Dhundhu and the Solar Dynasty
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of King Kuvalāśva (also known as Dhundhumāra) who, guided by sage Uttaṅka, kills the demon Dhundhu. The demon's fire breath kills all but three of Kuvalāśva's sons. Uttaṅka grants boons to the king. The lineage continues through Dṛḍhāśva, Haryaśva, Nikumbha, Saṃhatāśva, Akṛśāśva and Kṛśāśva, then Prasenajit, Yuvanāśva, and Māndhātā, who marries Bindumatī and fathers Purukutsa and Mucukunda. Purukutsa's son is Trasadasyu.

beats.title: The Exile and Redemption of Satyavrata
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana narrates the story of King Satyavrata, son of Trayyaruna, who was exiled by his father for abducting a maiden. He lived among outcasts, and during a twelve-year drought caused by his father's anger, he supported the family of the sage Vishvamitra by hunting and providing food. He also rescued Vishvamitra's son Galava from being sold. Despite his good deeds, Satyavrata harbored resentment towards the sage Vasistha for not intervening during his exile. The chapter highlights themes of dharma, karma, and the consequences of actions.

beats.title: The Story of Triśaṅku and the Lineage of Sagara
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana narrates the story of King Triśaṅku, who was cursed by his father and later helped by sage Viśvāmitra. It also traces the lineage of Triśaṅku's descendants, including Hariścandra, Rohita, Harita, Cañcu, Vijaya, Ruruka, Vṛka, Bāhu, and Sagara. Sagara defeats the Haihayas and Tālajaṅghas, and later abolishes the dharma of the Śakas, Pahlavas, and Pāradas.

beats.title: The Story of Sagara and the Descent of the Ganga
beats.summary: King Bahu loses his kingdom to the Haihayas and allied tribes, dies in exile. His pregnant queen is saved by sage Aurva, who performs rites for her son Sagara. Sagara grows up, defeats the invaders, but spares them at sage Vashistha's request, imposing cultural marks. He performs an Ashvamedha sacrifice; the horse is stolen near the ocean. Sagara's sons dig the earth, find the horse near Kapila (Vishnu), who burns all but four sons. Kapila grants a boon to the surviving descendants.

beats.title: The Lineage of Sagara and the Ikshvaku Dynasty
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana narrates the lineage of King Sagara, his two wives, the birth of his sixty thousand sons from a gourd, and the subsequent Ikshvaku dynasty down to Kalmashapada. It includes the boon from Aurva, the birth of the sons, and the genealogy of kings such as Bhagiratha, who brought the Ganga to earth.

beats.title: Ikshvaku Dynasty and the Origin of Soma
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana traces the Ikshvaku dynasty from Kalmashapada to Rama and his descendants, then shifts to the origin of Soma (the moon god) from Atri's tapas, his fall to earth, and his placement on a chariot by Brahma.

beats.title: The Origin of Soma and the Lunar Dynasty
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana narrates the origin of Soma (the moon god) and his lineage, including his birth from the mind of Brahma, his performance of the Rajasuya sacrifice, his abduction of Tara (Brihaspati's wife), the ensuing war between gods and demons (Tarakamaya war), the intervention of Brahma, the birth of Budha (Mercury) from Tara, and the birth of Pururavas from Budha and Viraj's daughter.

beats.title: The Lunar Dynasty: From Soma to the Sons of Kusha
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana narrates the lineage of Soma (the Moon) and his descendants, focusing on King Pururavas and his son Aila, then continuing through the dynasty to King Jahnu and his descendants, including the story of Jahnu drinking the Ganga and her subsequent adoption as his daughter (Jahnavi).

beats.title: The Birth of Gādhi and the Marriage of Satyavatī
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana narrates the story of King Kuśika's penance to obtain a son equal to Indra, leading to Indra himself being born as his son Gādhi. It then describes the marriage of Gādhi's daughter Satyavatī to the sage Ṛcīka, and the exchange of consecrated rice (caru) between mother and daughter, which results in Satyavatī giving birth to a fierce son (Jamadagni) and her mother to a peaceful son (Viśvāmitra). The chapter highlights themes of destiny, the power of tapas, and the interplay between kṣatriya and brāhmaṇa qualities.

beats.title: Lineages of Bhargavas and Kaushikas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana narrates the lineage of the Bhargava and Kaushika families, focusing on the births of Jamadagni, Vishvamitra, and their descendants. It details the story of Shunahshepha, who was adopted by Vishvamitra after being saved from sacrifice, and lists the many sons and gotras of Vishvamitra. The chapter also begins the lineage of Ayu, son of Pururavas.

beats.title: The Deception of King Raji
beats.summary: This chapter narrates the story of King Raji, who was asked by both devas and asuras to fight on their side in a cosmic war. Raji agreed to fight for the side that would make him Indra. The devas accepted, but the asuras refused. Raji then fought for the devas, killed the asuras, and was promised Indra's position. However, Indra tricked Raji by calling him 'father' and claiming to be his son, thus retaining his throne. After Raji's death, his sons tried to claim Indra's position but were killed by Indra due to their adharmic behavior.

beats.title: Genealogy of Kings and the Tale of Divodāsa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana (part 35) presents a genealogical list of kings and sages, tracing the lineage from Anenasa through various rulers including Pratikṣatra, Saṃjaya, Vijaya, Kṛti, Haryatvata, Sahadeva, Nadīna, Jayatsena, Saṃkṛti, Kṣatravṛddha, Sunahotra, Kāśa, Śala, Gṛtsamada, Śunaka, Śaunaka, Ārṣṭiseṇa, Kāśyapa, Kāśi, Dīrghatapas, Dhanvantari, Ketumān, Bhīmaratha, Divodāsa, Pratardana, Vatsa, Bharga, Alarka, and Saṃnati. It also recounts the story of Divodāsa conquering Vārāṇasī from Bhadraśreṇya and his son Durdama, and the birth of Pratardana.

beats.title: Lineages of Angirasa and Nahuṣa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana (part 36) traces the lineage of the Angirasa and Nahuṣa dynasties, listing kings from Alarka to Janamejaya, including the story of Yayāti and his sons, and the curse of Janamejaya.

beats.title: Yayāti's Division of the Earth and Exchange of Old Age
beats.summary: King Janamejaya seeks guidance from the sage Śaunaka, who performs an Aśvamedha sacrifice to purify him. The narrative then recounts the lineage of the chariot given by Indra to Bṛhadratha, which passes to Jarāsandha, is taken by Bhīma, and given to Vāsudeva. Yayāti conquers the earth, divides it among his five sons, and asks them to take his old age. Yadu, Druhya, Turvasu, and Anu refuse, incurring curses, while Puru accepts. Yayāti then enjoys youth and pleasures with Viśvācī.

beats.title: Yayati's Renunciation and the Puru Dynasty
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana narrates the story of King Yayati, who after enjoying worldly pleasures, realizes their futility and renounces them. He teaches about the insatiable nature of desire and the importance of detachment. The chapter then lists the descendants of Puru, including the birth of Soma from the sage Atri and Bhadra, and the story of Svarbhanu's attack on the sun.

beats.title: The Lineage of Atri and the Boons to Bali
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana traces the lineage of the Atri clan, starting from the sage Atri and his ten sons, the Svastyātreyas. It then follows the royal line through kings such as Kālānala, Sṛñjaya, Puraṃjaya, Janamejaya, Mahāśāla, Mahāmanā, Uśīnara, and Titikṣu, detailing their descendants and the founding of various dynasties and kingdoms. The chapter concludes with the story of Bali, who receives boons from Brahmā and becomes a great yogi.

beats.title: The Lineage of the Anga Dynasty and the Story of Bharata
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana narrates the lineage of the Anga dynasty, starting from King Bali and continuing through various kings including Dadhivahana, Diviratha, Dharmaratha, Citraratha, Lomapada (Daśaratha), Caturaṅga, Pṛthulākṣa, Campa, Haryaṅga, Bhadraratha, Bṛhatkarmā, Bṛhaddarbha, Bṛhanmanā, Jayadratha, Dṛḍharatha, Viśvajit, Janamejaya, Vaikarṇa, Vikarṇa, and others. It also mentions the lineage of Ṛceyu, including Matināra and his sons, and the story of Duṣyanta and Śakuntalā leading to Bharata. The chapter highlights the importance of dharma and the continuity of royal lineages.

beats.title: The Lineage of Angirasa: From Bharadvaja to Ajamidha
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana narrates the lineage of the Angirasa dynasty, starting from Bharadvaja and his son Vitatha, through the Kashi kings including Divodasa, Pratardana, Alarka, and others, up to the Ajamidha branch. It details the births, reigns, and key events such as the recovery of ancestral kingdom and the slaying of the demon Kshemaka.

beats.title: The Paurava Dynasty: From Ajamīḍha to Kuru
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana traces the lineage of the Paurava dynasty, focusing on the descendants of Ajamīḍha, including kings like Jahnu, Kuśika, Gādhi, Viśvāmitra, and the Somaka line leading to Drupada. It also narrates the story of Jahnu drinking the Ganges and later releasing her as Jāhnavī, and the birth of Ṛkṣa from Ajamīḍha and Dhūminī.

beats.title: The Kuru Dynasty Genealogy
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana traces the lineage of the Kuru dynasty from Ṛkṣa and Saṃvaraṇa down to the descendants of Parīkṣit, including the births of Bhīṣma, Dhṛtarāṣṭra, Pāṇḍu, Vidura, and the Pāṇḍavas. It also lists the sons of Somadatta and the later kings of the lunar dynasty.

beats.title: The Paurava Dynasty and the Story of Ajapārśva
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana narrates the story of Mālinī, a devoted wife who follows her husband after he abandons their newborn son. The son is rescued and named Ajapārśva. It then traces the Paurava dynasty from Yayāti's curse, listing kings including Duṣyanta, Karūroma, and others, and covers the lineages of Turvasu, Druhyu, Anu, and Yadu.

beats.title: The Lineage and Glory of Kārtavīrya Arjuna
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana narrates the lineage of the Haihaya dynasty, focusing on the mighty king Kārtavīrya Arjuna (Sahasrabāhu). It describes his birth, penance to please the sage Dattātreya, the boons he received (including a thousand arms, invincibility in battle, and righteous rule), his conquest of the seven continents, his performance of numerous sacrifices, and his extraordinary feats such as controlling the ocean's waves and subjugating the Nāgas.

beats.title: The Exploits and Fall of Kartavirya Arjuna
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana narrates the exploits of King Kartavirya Arjuna (Sahasrabahu), who had a thousand arms. It describes his playful yet destructive acts: churning the Narmada river with his arms, terrifying the serpent deities, capturing Ravana, and granting the seven islands as alms to Agni. It also recounts the curse by the sage Apava (Vasishtha) that leads to his death at the hands of Parasurama (Jamadagnya). The chapter ends with a genealogy of Kartavirya's sons, including Jayadhvaja and the Talajanghas.

beats.title: Yadava Genealogy
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana traces the lineage of the Yadava dynasty, starting from Jayadhvaja's son Tālajaṅgha, through the Haihaya and Yadava clans, down to Kṛṣṇa's ancestors. It details the descendants of Yadu, including Kroṣṭu, and the birth of Vṛṣṇi, Andhaka, and others. The narrative highlights the virtuous king Śvaphalka, who brought rain to Kāśi, and lists his sons, including Akrūra, and the sons of Citraka.

beats.title: The Vrishni Lineage and the Births of Heroes
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the lineage of the Vrishni clan, focusing on the descendants of Shura and the births of Vasudeva, his wives, and his children, including Krishna and his siblings. It also mentions the births of various kings and heroes, such as Shishupala and Dantavakra, and their connections to previous births.

beats.title: Genealogy of Vasudeva and the Birth of Kālayavana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana lists the wives and children of Vasudeva, including the birth of Krishna and Balarama, and narrates the story of Kālayavana's birth and the Vrishnis' flight to Dvārakā. It also includes a genealogy from Kroṣṭu to Citraratha.

beats.title: Yadava Dynasty Lineage
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana traces the lineage of the Yadava dynasty from Ushadgu through multiple generations, including kings like Citraratha, Shashabindu, Maruta, Kambalabarhis, Rukmakavaca, and others, culminating in Madhu and Purudvan. It recounts births, battles, and dynastic transitions, with a notable story of King Jyāmagha who, despite having no son, adopts a daughter-in-law and later begets Vidarbha through his wife's penance.

beats.title: Yadava Genealogy: From Madhu to Ahuka
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana (part 51) traces the lineage of the Yadava dynasty, focusing on the descendants of Satvata, including Bhajamana, Devavridha, and Andhaka. It narrates the birth of Babhru (Devavridha) from a river goddess, and the subsequent generations leading to Ahuka and Shrahuka.

beats.title: Genealogy of the Yadava Dynasty: Bhojas and Kukuras
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana (part 52) narrates the genealogy of the Yadava dynasty, focusing on the Bhoja and Kukura lineages. It lists kings, their descendants, and notable figures such as Ugrasena, Kamsa, and Satrajit, including the story of the Syamantaka jewel obtained from the sun god Surya.

beats.title: The Syamantaka Gem
beats.summary: King Satrajit receives the Syamantaka gem from the Sun god, gives it to his brother Prasena, who is killed by a lion while wearing it. The lion is killed by Jambavan, who takes the gem. Krishna is suspected of stealing it, so he tracks down Jambavan, enters his cave, and confronts him.

beats.title: The Syamantaka Gem: Krishna's Vindication and the Vrishni Genealogy
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana narrates the story of Krishna's vindication after being falsely accused of stealing the Syamantaka gem. It details Krishna's battle with Jambavan, his marriage to Jambavati, the recovery of the gem, and its return to Satrajit. The chapter also provides extensive genealogical information about the Vrishni dynasty, including the lineages of Satrajit, Shvaphalka, Akrura, and others.

beats.title: The Syamantaka Gem Episode
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana narrates the story of the Syamantaka gem. After Prasena is killed by a lion, Satrajit accuses Krishna of stealing the gem. Krishna pursues the thief Satadhanvan, kills him, but does not find the gem. Satadhanvan had given it to Akrura. Krishna's brother Balarama suspects Krishna of keeping the gem and leaves Dvārakā in anger. Meanwhile, Akrura performs many sacrifices. The chapter ends with Duryodhana visiting Mithila.

beats.title: The Syamantaka Gem and the Geography of the Earth
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana narrates the story of Akrura, who after killing Satrajit and taking the Syamantaka gem, leaves Dvaraka causing a drought. The Vrishnis bring him back, and Krishna, through yoga, learns of the gem's whereabouts. In a public assembly, Krishna asks Akrura to return the gem, but then gives it back to him. The chapter also includes a transition where the listener praises the previous narration and requests a description of the earth's geography, which the speaker begins by listing the seven continents.

beats.title: Geography of Jambudvipa and Mount Meru
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the geography of Jambudvipa, focusing on Mount Meru and its surrounding features: the seven continents, the central position of Jambudvipa, the dimensions of Meru, the four supporting mountains (Mandara, Gandhamadana, Vipula, Suparshva), the Jambu tree and its river Jambunadi, the four varsas (Bhadrashva, Ketumala, Ilavrita, etc.), the four forests (Chaitraratha, Gandhamadana, Vaibhraja, Nandana), the four lakes, and the subsidiary mountains (kesaracalas) in each direction.

beats.title: Geography of Jambudvipa and Avatars of Vishnu
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the geography of Jambudvipa, focusing on Mount Meru and its surrounding mountains, rivers, and regions. It details the four main divisions (Bhadrashva, Ketumala, Bharata, Kuru) and the avatars of Vishnu residing in each. The text also mentions the heavenly realms and the absence of sorrow in certain regions.

beats.title: Geography of Bharatavarsha
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the geography of Bharatavarsha (India), its nine divisions, major mountain ranges, rivers, and the people inhabiting different regions. It emphasizes that Bharatavarsha is the land of karma (action) where one can attain heaven, liberation, or other states through deeds, and where the four yugas (ages) occur.

beats.title: Geography of Jambūdvīpa and Plakṣadvīpa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the geography of Jambūdvīpa and Plakṣadvīpa, including their dimensions, mountains, rivers, and the characteristics of their inhabitants. It emphasizes the superiority of Bhārata (India) as a land of karma (action) leading to liberation, and contrasts it with other regions where beings enjoy the fruits of their actions. The text also introduces the seven sons of Medhātithi, rulers of Plakṣadvīpa, and describes the eternal Tretāyuga-like conditions in those islands.

beats.title: Cosmology of Plakṣa and Śālmalī Dvīpas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the geography and cosmology of Plakṣadvīpa and Śālmaladvīpa, including their mountains, rivers, and the four varṇas residing there, who worship Hari in various forms.

beats.title: Geography of Kuśadvīpa and Krauñcadvīpa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the geography of Kuśadvīpa and Krauñcadvīpa, including their mountains, rivers, and inhabitants. It details the seven varṣas (regions) of each dvīpa, the seven boundary mountains, and the seven major rivers. The chapter also mentions the worship of Janārdana (Viṣṇu) in various forms by the inhabitants.

beats.title: Geography of Śākadvīpa and Puṣkaradvīpa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the geography and features of Śākadvīpa and Puṣkaradvīpa, including rivers, mountains, and the characteristics of their inhabitants. It mentions the worship of Viṣṇu in the form of Sūrya, and describes the peaceful, disease-free life in these regions, with no duality or social hierarchies.

beats.title: Description of the Earth and Netherworlds
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the geography of the earth, including the seven continents (dvīpas) and oceans, the central mountain Meru, and the seven netherworlds (pātālas). It details the dimensions, inhabitants, and pleasures of the pātālas, emphasizing their beauty and the blissful life of the daityas, dānavas, and nāgas who reside there.

beats.title: Description of Shesha and the Hells
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the serpent Shesha (Ananta), who supports the earth and is a form of Vishnu's tamasic energy. It lists various hells (narakas) and the sins that lead to them.

beats.title: Enumeration of Sins and Corresponding Hells
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes various types of sinners and the corresponding hells (narakas) they are subjected to after death. It lists specific sins such as killing a Brahmana, stealing gold, selling liquor, violating vows, and others, along with the punishments in different hells like Taptakumbha, Krimibhakshya, Asipattravana, etc. The chapter emphasizes that those who act against the varṇāśrama dharma suffer in these hells, and the hierarchy of beings from immovables to liberated souls is outlined.

beats.title: The Power of Remembrance and Cosmic Dimensions
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana discusses the nature of sin, atonement, and the ultimate efficacy of remembering Vishnu (Krishna) for liberation. It contrasts heaven and hell as mental states, emphasizes that true atonement is repentance and remembrance of Hari, and describes the cosmic dimensions including the sun, moon, and stars.

beats.title: Cosmology of the Brahma Purana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the structure of the universe, including the arrangement of planets, stars, and the seven higher worlds (lokas) from Bhurloka to Satyaloka, as well as the seven lower worlds (patalas). It explains the dimensions and relative positions of these realms, the layers of elements surrounding the cosmic egg, and the role of primordial matter (pradhana) and Purusha, with Vishnu's power as the sustaining force.

beats.title: The Cosmic Manifestation and the Celestial Dolphin
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana explains the cosmic hierarchy and the role of Vishnu as the supreme cause. It uses analogies of a tree and seed to describe how the universe emanates from the unmanifest (avyakrita) through mahat, etc., and how all beings arise from Vishnu's power. It also describes the celestial form of Vishnu as the dolphin (śiśumāra) in the sky, with Dhruva at its tail, and explains the solar cycle of water absorption and rainfall.

beats.title: The Cosmic Water Cycle and the Importance of Inner Purity
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana discusses the origin and significance of water, particularly the celestial Ganga (Ākāśagaṅgā), and its role in purification and sustaining life. It emphasizes that true purification comes from inner purity rather than external rituals, and describes the cosmic hierarchy culminating in Nārāyaṇa as the ultimate support. The sages then ask about holy places (tīrthas) on earth.

beats.title: Enumeration of Sacred Tīrthas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana enumerates a long list of sacred tīrthas (pilgrimage sites) across India, including well-known ones like Puṣkara, Naimiṣāraṇya, Prayāga, Gayā, and many others. The text emphasizes their purifying power and spiritual significance.

beats.title: Enumeration of Tīrthas in Kurukṣetra
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana lists numerous sacred tīrthas (pilgrimage sites) in the region of Kurukṣetra, including rivers, ponds, forests, and other holy places. The enumeration emphasizes the spiritual merit of visiting these sites.

beats.title: Enumeration of Tirthas in the Brahma Purana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana enumerates a long list of sacred tirthas (pilgrimage sites), rivers, lakes, and hermitages, primarily in the region of the Sarasvati river. It includes names like aśvavedī, kubjāvana, yamunāprabhava, vīra, pramokṣa, sindhūttha, ṛṣakulyā, sakṛttika, urvīsaṃkramaṇa, māyāvidyodbhava, mahāśrama, vaitasikārūpa, sundarikāśrama, bāhutīrtha, cārunadī, vimalāśoka, pañcanada, mārkaṇḍeyasya dhīmataḥ, somatīrtha, sitoda, matsyodarī, sūryaprabha, sūryatīrtha, aśokavana, aruṇāspada, kāmada, śukratīrtha, savāluka, piśācamocana, subhadrāhrada, vimaladaṇḍa, caṇḍeśvara, jyeṣṭhasthānahrada, brahmasara, jaigīṣavyaguhā, harikeśavana, ajāmukhasara, ghaṇṭākarṇahrada, puṇḍarīkahrada, karkoṭaka, suvarṇasyodapāna, śvetatīrthahrada, ghargharikā, śyāmakūpa, candrikā, śmaśānastambhakūpa, vināyakāhrada, sindhūdbhava, rudrāvāsa, nāgatīrtha, pulomaka, bhaktahrada, kṣīrasara, pretādhāra, kumāraka, brahmāvarta, kuśāvarta, dadhikarṇodapāna, śṛṅgatīrtha, mahātīrtha, mahānadī, divya brahmasara, gayāśīrṣākṣaya vaṭa, dakṣiṇa, uttara, gomaya, rūpaśītika, kapilāhrada, gṛdhravaṭa, sāvitrīhrada, prabhāsana, sītavana, yonidvāra, dhenuka, dhanyaka, kokilākhya, mataṅgahrada, pitṛkūpa, rudratīrtha, śakratīrtha, sumālina, brahmasthāna, saptakuṇḍa, maṇiratnahrada, kauśikya, bharata, jyeṣṭhālikā, viśveśvara, kalpasara, kanyāsaṃvetya, niścīvā, prabhava, vasiṣṭhāśrama, devakūṭa, vīrāśrama, brahmavīrāvakāpilī, kumāradhārā, śrīdhārā, gaurīśikhara, śunaḥkuṇḍa, nanditīrtha, kumāravāsa, śrīvāsa, aurvīśītārtha, kumbhakarṇahrada, kauśikīhrada, dharmatīrtha, kāmatīrtha, uddālaka, saṃdhyātīrtha, kāratoya, kapila, lohitārṇava, śoṇodbhava, vaṃśagulma, ṛṣabha, kalatīrthaka, puṇyāvatīhrada, badarikāśrama, rāmatīrtha, pitṛvana, virajātīrtha, mārkaṇḍeyavana, kṛṣṇatīrtha, vaṭa, rohiṇīkūpa, indradyumnasara, sānugarta, samāhendra, śrītīrtha, śrīnada, iṣutīrtha, vārṣabha, kāverīhrada, kanyātīrtha, gokarṇa, gāyatrīsthāna, badarīhrada, madhyasthāna, vikarṇaka.

beats.title: The Gathering of Sages at Kuruksetra
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the greatness of various tirthas (sacred sites) and recounts a gathering of sages at Kuruksetra around Vyasa, who is asked about the best tirtha. The sages include many prominent rishis, and the scene is set in a beautiful ashram.

beats.title: The Kurukshetra War and the Glory of Bharata-varsha
beats.summary: The chapter begins with a dialogue between Dhritarashtra and Sanjaya about the Kurukshetra war, listing warriors on both sides. Then it shifts to a conversation between sages and Vyasa, who recounts a dialogue between sages and Brahma on Mount Meru. Brahma explains that Bharata-varsha (India) is the land of action (karmabhumi), where one performs deeds and attains results, including dharma, artha, kama, and moksha.

beats.title: The Glories of Bharata
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the glories of the Bharata region (India) as the land where dharma, artha, kama, and moksha can be attained. It lists the nine divisions of Bharata, its seven main mountain ranges, and numerous rivers. The chapter emphasizes that only in Bharata can humans perform actions leading to heaven or liberation, and even gods desire to be born there.

beats.title: Enumeration of Sacred Rivers and Regions
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana enumerates the sacred rivers and regions of India, describing their origins from various mountain ranges and their purifying qualities. It lists numerous rivers such as the Śoṇā, Narmadā, Godāvarī, and Kṛṣṇaveṇā, and categorizes them by their sources like the Vindhya, Sahya, and Malayādri mountains. The text also names various janapadas (tribal regions) including the Matsyas, Kuntalas, Kāśis, Kośalas, Andhras, Kaliṅgas, Gāndhāras, Yavanas, and many others, providing a geographical and cultural overview of ancient Bhāratavarṣa.

beats.title: The Glories of Bharata Varsha
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the various janapadas (regions) of Bharata Varsha, including eastern, southern, western, and northern territories, as well as those dwelling in the Vindhya mountains. It extols the uniqueness of Bharata as the land of karma, where beings can attain the fruits of dharma, artha, kama, and moksha through righteous actions, and where even gods desire human birth.

beats.title: Glories of Bharata Varsha and Konaditya
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the glories of the Bharata varsha (India), especially the Odra desha (Odisha) region, and the sacred sun temple of Konaditya (Konark). It extols the virtues of the land, its people, and the benefits of visiting the temple, particularly during the month of Magha.

beats.title: The Ritual of Offering Arghya to the Sun
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the ritual of offering arghya (water libation) to the Sun god (Surya). It details the procedure, including purification, drawing a lotus diagram, offering ingredients, meditation on Surya's form, and the mantra recitation. It emphasizes that this practice grants various desires and is a prerequisite for worshiping Vishnu or Shiva.

beats.title: The Glories of Konarka and Rameshvara
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the sacred site of Konarka (the Sun temple) on the seashore, detailing the rituals and benefits of bathing in the ocean, offering arghya to the sun, worshipping the sun god and Rameshvara (Shiva), and performing pilgrimages. It emphasizes the fruits of such acts, including attainment of sun's world, rebirth in a yogi family, and eventual liberation through yoga.

beats.title: The Nature of Devotion to the Sun God
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana (part 82) describes the nature of devotion (bhakti) to the Sun god (Bhaskara), including practices such as fasting, offerings, circumambulation, and recitation. It emphasizes purity of mind, faith, and meditation as essential components of worship, and outlines the fruits of such devotion, including liberation from sins and attainment of the solar realm.

beats.title: The Ādityavrata and Vijaya Saptamī
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the rituals and benefits of observing the Ādityavrata, particularly the Vijaya Saptamī vow dedicated to the Sun god. It details the procedures for offering arghya, lighting lamps, and performing worship, emphasizing the purification from sins and attainment of desired results.

beats.title: The Glory of the Sun God
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana (part 84) extols the greatness of the Sun god (Surya/Aditya). It describes the benefits of worshiping the Sun with various offerings, such as ghee, milk, curd, and items like umbrellas and flags. The Sun is declared the supreme deity, the creator and destroyer of the universe, and the ultimate goal for yogis. The chapter also includes a dialogue where a seeker asks about the path to liberation and the identity of the highest god, and the answer identifies the Sun as that supreme being.

beats.title: The Twelve Forms of the Sun and the Path to Liberation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the twelve forms of the Sun god (Surya) and their functions. It explains how sages, kings, and householders attained liberation through yoga and devotion to the Sun. The Sun is identified as the supreme deity, and his twelve manifestations (Indra, Dhata, Parjanya, Tvashta, Pusha, Aryama, Bhaga, Vivasvan, Vishnu, Amshuman, Varuna, Mitra) govern various aspects of the universe.

beats.title: Mitra's Tapas and the Nature of the Self
beats.summary: Narada asks Mitra (the Sun) why he performs tapas despite being the supreme deity. Mitra explains the nature of the supreme self (Paramatman) as the inner witness, beyond the three gunas, and the source of all. He reveals that he performs tapas as an example for devotees.

beats.title: The Discourse on the Supreme Self
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana (part 87) is a discourse on the nature of the Supreme Self (Paramatman) as the inner witness of all beings, the all-pervading, formless, and yet manifesting in myriad forms. It explains how the one Purusha becomes many through the power of maya, and how the same Self is worshipped in various forms, especially as the Sun (Divakara). The teaching emphasizes that the Self is beyond gunas, yet appears as the universe, and that devotion to the Self leads to liberation.

beats.title: The Glory of the Sun
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the supreme importance of the Sun god (Surya/Aditya) as the source of all creation, sustenance, and dissolution. It explains how the Sun governs time, seasons, and life cycles, and lists the twelve names of Aditya (the twelve suns) associated with each month. The text emphasizes that meditation on the Sun leads to liberation and that the Sun is the ultimate deity.

beats.title: The Twelve Forms of the Sun and the Hymn of Twenty-One Names
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the twelve forms of the Sun god (Surya) corresponding to the twelve months, their names, and the rays associated with each. It also narrates the origin of Surya from the sage Kashyapa and his wives Aditi, Diti, Danu, and Vinata, explaining how the Sun's rays manifest differently in different seasons. The chapter includes a hymn of 21 names of Surya and emphasizes the purifying power of reciting it.

beats.title: Aditi's Penance and the Sun's Boon
beats.summary: Aditi, distressed by the Daityas and Danavas usurping her sons' dominion and sacrificial shares, performs severe penance and prays to the Sun god (Vivasvan). The Sun appears and grants her a boon: he will be born as her son to destroy the enemies and restore the gods' position.

beats.title: The Birth of Martanda and the Victory over Asuras
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana narrates the birth of Martanda (the Sun god) from Aditi through the grace of Surya, his emergence from the egg, the battle with the Asuras, and his marriage to Samjna. It also describes the initial form of the Sun and the birth of his children.

beats.title: The Story of Samjna and Chhaya
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana narrates the story of Surya's wife Samjna, who, unable to bear his intense heat, creates a shadow (Chhaya) to take her place and goes to her father's house. Chhaya bears children to Surya but treats her stepchildren unfairly, leading to a curse from Yama. Surya discovers the deception and reassures Yama that the curse will be mitigated.

beats.title: The Reduction of Surya's Effulgence
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the story of Surya (Sun god) and his wife Samjna. Unable to bear Surya's intense brilliance, Samjna leaves him and performs penance in the forest. Surya, angered, is pacified by Brahma and others. At Brahma's instruction, Vishvakarma reduces Surya's effulgence by placing him on a lathe, causing cosmic disturbances. The gods and sages praise Surya, and the Gandharvas sing to him.

beats.title: The Origin and Glorification of the Sun God
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the creation of the sun god Surya, his depiction by Vishvakarma, and the praise of Surya as the supreme deity. It includes a dialogue where sages ask about the origin of the sun's brilliance, leading to a cosmogonic account of creation from primordial darkness, the emergence of the cosmic egg, and the realization of Surya as the supreme self. The chapter ends with a hymn identifying Surya with various gods and cosmic principles.

beats.title: Hymn to the Sun and the 108 Names
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana (part 95) is a hymn of praise to the Sun god (Surya/Arka/Bhaskara). It describes the supreme, all-pervading nature of the deity, who is identified with the ultimate reality (Parameshvara). The sages or devotees praise the Sun's cosmic form, seek boons, and are granted that the Sun's form be made bearable for the world's welfare. The chapter then lists the 108 names of the Sun, emphasizing the power of devotion and the recitation of these names for liberation from sin and attainment of heaven.

beats.title: The 108 Names of the Sun and the Destruction of Daksha's Sacrifice
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana presents a hymn of 108 names of the Sun (Surya) and narrates the story of Daksha's sacrifice and its destruction by Rudra (Shiva). It begins with a list of Surya's names and attributes, then transitions to a question about why Rudra destroyed Daksha's sacrifice. The answer reveals that Daksha, harboring enmity towards Rudra, did not invite his daughter Sati (Rudra's wife) to the sacrifice, leading to Rudra's wrath.

beats.title: The Insult and Self-Immolation of Sati
beats.summary: Daksha insults his daughter Sati, who is married to Shiva, by favoring her younger sisters and their husbands. Sati, enraged, immolates herself. Shiva learns of this and curses Daksha to be reborn as a mortal king, while Daksha in turn curses Shiva to be excluded from sacrifices.

beats.title: The Tale of Daksha and Rudra: Sati's Sacrifice and Rebirth
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana recounts the story of Daksha and Rudra's conflict, the birth of Sati and her marriage to Shiva, and the subsequent events leading to Sati's self-immolation and rebirth as Uma. It also discusses the nature of karma and the importance of not holding grudges across lifetimes.

beats.title: The Birth of Ganga and the Power of Progeny
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the story of a mountain king (Shailendra) who, after being advised by a sage, performs severe penance to obtain a son. Lord Shiva appears and grants him a daughter instead, who becomes the sacred river Ganga. The story emphasizes the importance of progeny for liberation of ancestors and the power of penance.

beats.title: The Penance of the Three Kumaris and Uma's Marriage to Shiva
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana narrates the penance of the three kumaris (virgins) – Ekaparna, Ekapatala, and Aparna (Uma) – daughters of Himavan. They perform severe austerities, subsisting on single leaves. Uma, the youngest, gives up food entirely, causing her mother to forbid her, leading to her name Aparna (leafless). The three maidens are praised as mothers of the world. Uma, the foremost, is destined to marry Shiva. The gods inform her that Dhurjati (Shiva) will be her husband. Later, Shiva approaches her in a deformed guise.

beats.title: Shiva's Disguise and Parvati's Svayamvara
beats.summary: Shiva, disguised as a deformed Brahmin, approaches Parvati and asks for her hand. Parvati, recognizing him, chooses him in a svayamvara. Shiva blesses the ashoka tree and the Citrakuta hermitage, granting immortality and spiritual benefits to those who visit or die there.

beats.title: Parvati's Sacrifice for the Boy
beats.summary: In this chapter from the Brahma Purana, the goddess Parvati (Shailaja) hears the cries of a boy being attacked by a crocodile in a lake. She pleads with the crocodile to release the boy, offering her own accumulated merit (tapas) in exchange. The crocodile, revealed to be a divine being, accepts her offer and releases the boy, then transforms into a radiant form. The chapter highlights themes of sacrifice, compassion, and the power of tapas.

beats.title: Parvati's Penance and Svayamvara
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the story of Goddess Parvati's penance, her encounter with a crocodile (grāha) who tests her resolve, and her eventual liberation of a boy. Pleased with her devotion, Shiva grants her the boon of inexhaustible penance. Later, her father Himavan organizes a svayamvara for her, inviting all gods, including Indra, Surya, and others.

beats.title: The Svayamvara of the Goddess and the Child Shiva
beats.summary: The chapter describes the gathering of gods and beings for a svayamvara (self-choice ceremony) of the goddess, who is the supreme Prakriti and wife of Shiva. Various deities arrive in celestial vehicles, including Brahma, Vishnu, Indra, and others. The goddess, seeking to test Shiva, sees him as a five-tufted child on her lap. The gods, confused and angered, try to attack the child but are paralyzed by Shiva's power.

beats.title: Brahma's Intervention and the Marriage of Shiva and Parvati
beats.summary: Brahma describes to the sages how he approached Shiva and Parvati to request the restoration of the gods who had been blinded by Shiva's third eye. After propitiating Shiva with hymns, Shiva calmed down and granted the gods divine vision. Brahma then arranged the marriage of Shiva and Parvati, with Himavan's consent.

beats.title: The Wedding of Shiva and Parvati
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the preparations for the wedding of Lord Shiva (Mahesha) and Parvati (Giriduhitri). The gods, sages, and celestial beings gather in a magnificent city built by the sages. The chapter vividly depicts the beauty of the city, the arrival of various beings, and the presence of nature in a state of celebration, including clouds, peacocks, and rivers. The rainy season is personified as a beautiful woman, symbolizing the auspiciousness of the event.

beats.title: The Seasons Attend Parvati's Wedding
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the arrival of various seasons and the natural beauty of the Himalayas during the wedding of Parvati (the daughter of the mountain) and Shiva. It vividly portrays the landscape, flora, fauna, and the harmonious atmosphere of the divine event.

beats.title: The Marriage of Shiva and Parvati
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the beauty of the Himalayas during the wedding of Shiva and Parvati. It details the lush flora, fauna, and the arrival of the six seasons. The sage recounts how he performed the marriage ceremony, binding their hands and offering oblations into the fire.

beats.title: Marriage of Shiva and Parvati
beats.summary: The chapter describes the marriage of Shiva and Parvati, including the fire sacrifice, the circumambulation, and the hymns of praise by the gods led by Indra. It details the various forms and attributes of Shiva as praised by the deities.

beats.title: The Burning of Kama and the Boon to Rati
beats.summary: The chapter describes the burning of Kama (Manmatha) by Shiva's third eye, Rati's lamentation, and the boon that Kama will be reborn as Pradyumna, son of Krishna and Rukmini. It also includes hymns to Shiva and the benefits of reciting the Harotsava.

beats.title: Shiva and Parvati's Himalayan Pastimes
beats.summary: Shiva and Parvati reside on Mount Himavat, enjoying divine pastimes. Parvati visits her mother Menā, who criticizes Shiva's poverty and unconventional lifestyle. Hurt, Parvati asks Shiva to move elsewhere. Shiva laughs and explains his ascetic nature, but Parvati insists on leaving.

beats.title: The Assembly on Meru and the Prelude to Daksha's Sacrifice
beats.summary: This chapter describes the gathering of gods, sages, and other beings around Mahadeva (Shiva) on Mount Meru, and introduces the story of Daksha's sacrifice which Mahadeva will later destroy due to an insult to his wife Sati.

beats.title: Daksha's Sacrifice and Shiva's Exclusion
beats.summary: Daksha performs a horse sacrifice but does not invite Shiva. The sage Dadhichi criticizes Daksha for not honoring Shiva, the lord of all beings. Shiva's wife Sati questions Shiva about the gods going to the sacrifice, and Shiva explains that he is not invited and no share is allotted to him. Sati suggests various penances to secure a share for Shiva, but Shiva reveals his supreme nature and that he is the true recipient of all sacrifices.

beats.title: Destruction of Daksha's Sacrifice
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the destruction of Daksha's sacrifice by Virabhadra and the Rudra ganas, sent by Shiva in response to the insult to his wife Sati (Uma). The ganas wreak havoc, terrifying the gods and destroying the sacrificial grounds.

beats.title: Destruction of Daksha's Sacrifice
beats.summary: This chapter from the Brahma Purana describes the destruction of Daksha's sacrifice by Virabhadra and Bhadrakali, born from the wrath of Shiva and Devi. The gods and sages seek refuge in Shiva, who eventually pacifies the chaos and grants Daksha a share in the sacrifice. The narrative includes the origin of Jvara (fever) from a sweat drop of Shiva's gana.

beats.title: Daksha's Praise of Shiva
beats.summary: Daksha, terrified and tearful, seeks a boon from Shiva that the sacrificial materials he had destroyed be restored. Shiva grants it. Daksha then praises Shiva with a thousand names, describing his cosmic form and attributes.

beats.title: Hymn to Rudra
beats.summary: This chapter is a hymn of praise to Rudra/Shiva, listing his various forms, attributes, and epithets. It emphasizes his role as creator, destroyer, and sustainer, and his presence in all beings. The verses are a series of salutations (namas) to different aspects of Shiva.
